title: Anthropic $1.5 billion copyright settlement approved by judge
excerpt: A federal judge signed off on the payout Monday, which works out to $3,000 per work across an estimated 500,000 titles
content_html:
Cris Tolomia

Tue, July 21, 2026 at 7:57 AM EDT 2 min read

Anthropic's $1.5 billion settlement of a class action copyright lawsuit brought by authors and publishers received final court approval on Monday. U.S. District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin, sitting in the Northern District of California, signed off on the agreement, which is believed to be the largest settlement in the history of U.S. copyright law, according to Reuters.

The settlement covers an estimated 500,000 works and will pay $3,000 per work to the authors and publishers who hold rights to them. Judge Martinez-Olguin rejected arguments that the amount was too small, according to Reuters.

The lawsuit stemmed from accusations that Anthropic used copyrighted books without permission to train its AI chatbot Claude. A previous judge on the case, U.S. District Judge William Alsup, had ruled that training an AI model on copyrighted text constitutes fair use — a decision widely seen as significant for the broader AI industry, according to TechCrunch. Judge Alsup, who has since retired, had also found that Anthropic illegally obtained a portion of its training library by downloading books from pirate sites, including Library Genesis and Pirate Library Mirror, rather than purchasing them.

With Judge Alsup having determined the piracy issue was triable, Anthropic moved to settle rather than face a jury and the possibility of substantial damages. Because Anthropic chose to settle rather than appeal, Judge Alsup's fair use determination will never be reviewed by a higher court and carries no industry-wide legal force, according to TechCrunch.

Not all parties joined the settlement. Some authors and publishers opted out and are continuing separate cases against Anthropic, according to Reuters.

The resolution does not end broader litigation over AI and copyright. Lawsuits remain active against companies including Google, Meta, Midjourney, and OpenAI over whether training AI models on copyrighted works is legal. Just days ago, publishers Hachette, Cengage, and Elsevier joined author Scott Turow and advocacy group S.C.R.I.B.E. in bringing a fresh class action against Google, alleging the company trained its Gemini AI on their copyrighted material without authorization.

content_html_zh:
克里斯·托洛米亚

2026 年 7 月 21 日星期二上午 7:57（美国东部时间） 阅读 2 分钟

Anthropic 以 15 亿美元解决由作者和出版商提起的版权集体诉讼，周一获得了法院的最终批准。 据路透社报道，加利福尼亚州北区的美国地区法官阿拉塞利·马丁内斯-奥尔金签署了该协议，该协议被认为是美国版权法历史上最大的和解协议。

和解协议涵盖约 500,000 件作品，并将向拥有这些作品权利的作者和出版商支付每件作品 3,000 美元的费用。 据路透社报道，法官马丁内斯-奥尔金驳回了金额太小的论点。

该诉讼源于指控 Anthropic 在未经许可的情况下使用受版权保护的书籍来训练其人工智能聊天机器人 Claude。 据 TechCrunch 报道，该案的前任法官美国地区法官 William Alsup 曾裁定，在受版权保护的文本上训练人工智能模型构成合理使用，这一决定被广泛认为对更广泛的人工智能行业具有重要意义。 现已退休的阿尔苏普法官还发现，Anthropic 通过从盗版网站（包括 Library Genesis 和 Pirate Library Mirror）下载书籍而不是购买书籍，非法获得了其培训图书馆的一部分。

随着阿尔苏普法官确定盗版问题可以审理，Anthropic 决定和解，而不是面对陪审团和可能造成的重大损失。 据 TechCrunch 报道，由于 Anthropic 选择和解而不是上诉，阿尔苏普法官的合理使用裁决永远不会受到更高一级法院的审查，也不具有全行业的法律效力。

并非所有各方都加入了和解协议。 据路透社报道，一些作者和出版商选择退出，并继续针对 Anthropic 提起单独的诉讼。

该决议并没有结束有关人工智能和版权的更广泛的诉讼。 针对谷歌、Meta、Midjourney 和 OpenAI 等公司的诉讼仍然活跃，涉及在受版权保护的作品上训练人工智能模型是否合法。 就在几天前，出版商阿歇特 (Hachette)、圣智 (Cengage) 和爱思唯尔 (Elsevier) 加入了作家斯科特·图罗 (Scott Turow) 和倡导团体 S.C.R.I.B.E. 的行列。 对谷歌提起新的集体诉讼，指控该公司未经授权使用其受版权保护的材料训练 Gemini AI。
